_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NICE CKS Lichen Planus, JAMA Dermatology consensus, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Lichen Planus: Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Lichen planus is a chronic, inflammatory, immune-mediated disorder that affects the skin, mucous membranes, hair, and nails. The characteristic presentation on skin is the '6 Ps': Purple, Polygonal, Planar (flat-topped), Pruritic Papules and Plaques. The pathophysiology involves a T-cell-mediated attack on basal keratinocytes, triggered by unknown antigens that may be induced by viral infections (particularly Hepatitis C), drugs, or contact allergens. Lichen planus affects approximately 0.5-2% of the global population, with peak incidence in middle-aged adults. The condition is self-limited in most patients — skin lesions typically resolve within 1-2 years — but mucosal involvement (oral and genital) and hypertrophic variants can be chronic and difficult to treat. Modern treatments including topical and intralesional corticosteroids, retinoids, and JAK inhibitors can effectively manage symptoms in most patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 0.5-2% of the global population; likely underdiagnosed given the variable presentation
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in middle-aged adults (30-60); women are slightly more commonly affected; rare in children
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset is 40-50 years; oral lichen planus typically presents in the 5th-6th decade

definition: Lichen planus (ICD-10: L43) is a chronic, inflammatory, immune-mediated disorder affecting the skin, mucous membranes, hair follicles, and nails. The pathophysiology involves a CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell-mediated attack on basal keratinocytes and mucosal epithelial cells — this cytotoxic attack is triggered by antigenic changes in the epithelium that may be induced by viral infections (Hepatitis C), drugs, contact allergens, or autoantigens. Histologically, the condition shows a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction, 'sawtooth' irregular acanthosis, and civatte bodies (apoptotic keratinocytes). The clinical manifestations range from classic skin papules to oral ulceration, nail dystrophy, and scarring alopecia. The condition is managed by dermatologists; oral lichen planus may require dermatologist-oral medicine co-management.

typesClassification.description: The classic presentation: purple, polygonal, flat-topped, pruritic papules and plaques, often with Wickham's striae (white, lacelike reticular pattern on surface). Distribution is typically flexural surfaces, wrists, ankles, and lumbar region. Koebner phenomenon (lesions in trauma sites) is characteristic.

Thick, hyperkeratotic, often pruritic plaques, typically on the shins. This variant is more chronic and refractory to treatment than classic lichen planus. Can lead to post-inflammatory hypopigmentation. Associated with a higher risk of squamous cell carcinoma.

Affects the oral mucosa — reticular (white, lacy streaks most common), atrophic (red, atrophic patches), erosive (ulcerated, painful areas), and bullous (subepithelial blisters, rare) forms. Most commonly affects the buccal mucosa, tongue, and gingiva. Erosovular and erosive forms are painful and can impair eating. Associated with Hepatitis C in endemic areas. Malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma is a documented risk (0.5-2% over decades).

Affects the vulva, vagina, or penis. In women: white, lacy lesions (Wickham's striae) or erosive, painful lesions on the vulva. Can cause scarring (vulvar architectural changes) and sexual dysfunction. Requires regular monitoring for squamous cell carcinoma risk.

A scarring alopecia affecting the scalp. Presents with perifollicular erythema, scaling, and follicular papules that progress to scarring and permanent hair loss. The inflammatory process destroys hair follicles. Active disease shows perifollicular erythema and 'follicular plugs'; end-stage shows smooth, scarred scalp without follicles.

Affects the nails with pitting, ridging, splitting, and thinning. Can cause permanent nail dystrophy and scarring (pterygium formation — dorsal nail fusion to the nail bed). Can affect single or multiple nails. Involvement of all 20 nails (twenty-nail dystrophy) in children is often lichen planus.

A variant presenting with brown-gray macules in sun-exposed and flexural areas, particularly in individuals with darker skin (South Asian, Middle Eastern, Latin American). Usually asymptomatic. Lacks the classic papular component.

primarySymptoms: Pruritic, purple, polygonal, flat-topped papules and plaques — the classic presentation. The '6 Ps': Purple, Polygonal, Planar, Pruritic Papules and Plaques. Lesions are often intensely itchy — pruritus is often the most bothersome symptom. Wicksham's striae (white, lacy network) may be visible on papule surfaces.
Koebner phenomenon — new lesions appearing in areas of skin trauma (scratch marks, surgical scars, areas of friction). This is characteristic of lichen planus and helps confirm the diagnosis.
Oral lesions — white, lacy (reticular) streaks on buccal mucosa and tongue are the most common form; painful erosive lesions may make eating, drinking, and oral hygiene difficult.

Nail changes — pitting, ridging, splitting, nail thinning, onycholysis (separation from nail bed), and in severe cases, permanent dystrophy or pterygium (scarring where the nail is replaced by a scar).
Scalp involvement (lichen planopilaris) — perifollicular erythema and scaling, progressive scarring alopecia. Patients notice hair loss that is often permanent if not treated early.
Hypertrophic plaques on shins — thick, hyperkeratotic plaques on the lower legs, often intensely pruritic and potentially ulcerating.
Mucosal involvement (genital) — white streaks or erosive, painful lesions on genitalia; can cause scarring and sexual dysfunction in women and men.
earlyWarningSigns: New purple, flat-topped papules appearing in characteristic distribution (wrists, forearms, ankles, lower back) — particularly if intensely pruritic
White, lacy streaks on the inside of the cheeks or tongue — oral lichen planus often presents as the first manifestation
New nail ridging, splitting, or dystrophy in an adult — particularly if multiple nails are affected
Patchy hair loss with perifollicular erythema on the scalp — may indicate lichen planopilaris
Koebner phenomenon — lesions appearing in scratch marks or trauma sites
emergencySigns: Extensive oral mucosal involvement with severe pain — erosive oral lichen planus can cause severe pain impairing eating and hydration, requiring urgent management
Extensive bullous lesions — bullous lichen planus with extensive blistering requires urgent evaluation
Signs of secondary infection — weeping, crusted lesions with warmth and fever suggesting bacterial infection
Eye involvement — ocular lichen planus (rare) can cause chronic conjunctivitis and scarring; requires urgent ophthalmology referral

Severe mucosal involvement suggesting early squamous cell carcinoma — non-healing erosions, induration, or ulceration in long-standing oral or genital lichen planus requires biopsy to rule out malignant transformation

causes.description: The primary mechanism. CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes are recruited to the dermoepidermal junction (skin) or basal epithelial layer (mucosa), where they target and destroy basal keratinocytes or epithelial cells. This is triggered by unknown antigenic changes in the epithelium — possibly induced by viral infections (Hepatitis C), drugs, contact allergens, or autoantigens. The attack is Th1-skewed with IFN-gamma and IL-2 as key cytokines. The band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction is the histological hallmark.

Chronic Hepatitis C infection is a well-established trigger for lichen planus, particularly in regions with high HCV prevalence (Mediterranean, East Asia, North Africa). HCV may trigger lichen planus through chronic antigenic stimulation, immune complex deposition, or molecular mimicry. All patients with lichen planus should be screened for HCV in endemic areas; the association is less clear in low-prevalence regions.

A significant number of lichen planus cases are drug-induced or drug-exacerbated. Implicated drugs include: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, sulfonylureas, gold salts, antimalarials, beta-blockers, and proton pump inhibitors. Drug-induced LP typically presents with a widespread, more eczematous or lichenoid pattern and resolves after drug withdrawal. A thorough medication history is essential.

Oral lichen planus can be triggered or exacerbated by contact allergens in dental materials — particularly amalgam dental fillings (mercury, silver). A subset of patients with oral LP improves after removal of amalgam restorations. Patch testing by a dermatologist or allergist can identify relevant contact allergens.

Familial lichen planus occurs in approximately 1-2% of cases and suggests genetic contribution. HLA associations include HLA-DR1 (increased risk), HLA-DR6, and HLA-DR10 in various populations. The genetic contribution is modest — most cases are sporadic.

Other viral infections have been implicated in lichen planus pathogenesis: Hepatitis B, HIV, HPV (particularly in oral LP), and EBV. The evidence is strongest for Hepatitis C; other viral associations are less consistently confirmed.

riskFactors.description: Peak incidence is in middle-aged adults. The condition is uncommon in children and young adults (except when drug-induced). Prevalence decreases in older age groups.
Women are slightly more commonly affected than men, particularly for oral and genital lichen planus. This may relate to hormonal factors and differences in immune response.
Chronic Hepatitis C infection is an established trigger for lichen planus, particularly in endemic regions. Hepatitis C patients have approximately 3-5x increased risk of lichen planus.
Drug-induced lichen planus is triggered by a wide range of medications including NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides, PPIs, and antimalarials. A medication history review is essential in any new case.

For oral lichen planus, amalgam dental restorations (containing mercury, silver) can trigger or exacerbate the condition in a subset of patients. Replacement with composite or ceramic materials can improve symptoms in affected patients.
Contact allergy to certain substances (cosmetics, fragrances, nickel, certain medications applied topically) can trigger or exacerbate lichen planus. Patch testing can identify relevant allergens.

affectedDemographics: Lichen planus affects approximately 0.5-2% of the global population — an estimated 40-100 million people worldwide
Peak incidence is in middle-aged adults (30-60 years); the condition is uncommon in children (except in certain drug-induced or familial cases)
Women are slightly more commonly affected than men, particularly for oral and genital lichen planus
Oral lichen planus is the most common form of mucosal lichen planus, affecting approximately 0.5-2% of the general population
Lichen planopilaris (follicular LP) is a significant cause of scarring alopecia, accounting for approximately 5-10% of cases in specialized hair loss clinics
Lichen planus pigmentosus is particularly common in individuals with darker skin tones from South Asia, Middle East, and Latin America
diagnosisOverview: Lichen planus diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on the characteristic appearance of the '6 Ps': Purple, Polygonal, Planar, Pruritic Papules and Plaques, often with Wickham's striae (white lacy network on papule surface). Koebner phenomenon (lesions at trauma sites) supports the diagnosis. A thorough history should cover: medication use, dental history (for oral LP), Hepatitis C risk factors, and family history. Skin biopsy confirms the diagnosis in atypical or persistent cases — histopathology shows: band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction, 'sawtooth' irregular acanthosis, and civatte bodies (apoptotic keratinocytes). For oral lichen planus, biopsy is recommended to rule out dysplasia and squamous cell carcinoma in erosive/atrophic lesions. All patients with oral LP should have a baseline biopsy of erosive areas and regular monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone of diagnosis. Classic skin lesions (purple, polygonal, flat-topped papules with Wickham's striae, Koebner phenomenon) establish the diagnosis. History should cover medications, dental history, and Hepatitis C risk factors.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination of skin, scalp, nails, and oral mucosa. A dermatologist will assess lesion morphology, distribution, and severity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis in atypical presentations, persistent cases, or when other conditions are in the differential. Histopathology shows: band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction (interface dermatitis), 'sawtooth' irregular acanthosis, and civatte bodies (apoptotic keratinocytes). Direct immunofluorescence may be used in some cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4mm punch biopsy under local anesthesia; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening for Hepatitis C is recommended for all patients with lichen planus in endemic regions, given the established association. Hepatitis C triggers lichen planus through chronic antigenic stimulation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for HCV antibodies and if positive, HCV RNA PCR. Results within 1 week.

diagnosticTests.purpose: For erosive or atrophic oral lichen planus, baseline biopsy is recommended to rule out squamous cell carcinoma and assess for dysplasia. Long-standing erosive oral LP has a small but real risk (0.5-2%) of malignant transformation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Punch or incisional biopsy of representative erosive lesion under local anesthesia; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: For oral lichen planus possibly related to dental materials or for lichen planus possibly triggered by contact allergens, patch testing by a dermatologist or allergist can identify relevant allergens.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard patch test series applied to the back; readings at 48 and 72 hours. Identifies contact allergens contributing to the condition.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for lichen planus depends on the site, severity, and variant. For mild cutaneous lichen planus: potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment), intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone) for individual papules, and topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus). For moderate-to-severe or widespread cutaneous LP: oral antihistamines for pruritus, oral corticosteroids (short courses for acute flares), oral retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin), and methotrexate for refractory cases. For oral lichen planus: topical corticosteroids (clobetasol gel or mouthwash), topical calcineurin inhibitors, and for severe cases, systemic corticosteroids or other immunosuppressants. For lichen planopilaris: topical and intralesional corticosteroids, hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline (anti-inflammatory dose), and in refractory cases, JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib). For hypertrophic LP: intralesional corticosteroids, topical retinoids, and in refractory cases, systemic therapy. All treatments manage symptoms — there is no cure, and the condition may require long-term maintenance therapy for mucosal and follicular variants.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical high-potency corticosteroids (Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment/cream)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for localized cutaneous lichen planus. Potent topical steroids reduce local inflammation and pruritus. Applied twice daily to affected areas. May cause skin atrophy with prolonged use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for localized disease — 60-80% achieve significant improvement in 4-8 weeks. Less effective for widespread or hypertrophic disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional corticosteroid injections (Triamcinolone acetonide 10-40mg/ml)
medicalTreatments.description: For individual papules or plaques, particularly hypertrophic lesions on the shins. Triamcinolone is injected into the lesion, reducing inflammation and thickness. Sessions repeated every 4-6 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for isolated, hypertrophic lesions — 70-80% improvement. Less practical for widespread disease. Can cause local atrophy.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors — Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, Pimecrolimus 1% cream
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents particularly useful for facial lichen planus (avoiding steroid atrophy) and for oral lichen planus (gel or mouthwash formulations). Apply twice daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for cutaneous and mucosal LP — 60-70% improvement. Avoids steroid side effects. May cause burning on application.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day tapered over 2-4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: For severe, extensive, or rapidly progressive cutaneous LP; for severe erosive oral LP; and for lichen planopilaris with rapid hair loss. Rapidly controls inflammation and symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid and effective — symptoms improve within days. Used short-term to control acute flares. Long-term use avoided due to steroid side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral retinoids — Acitretin 25-30mg daily or Isotretinoin 0.5mg/kg daily
medicalTreatments.description: For widespread, hypertrophic, or refractory lichen planus. Retinoids normalize keratinocyte differentiation and have anti-inflammatory effects. Particularly useful for hypertrophic and generalized LP.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for hypertrophic and widespread LP — 60-70% improvement. Teratogenic (acitretin); requires contraception. Requires monitoring for hepatotoxicity and lipid elevation.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 200-400mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: For lichen planopilaris and erosive oral lichen planus. An immunomodulatory agent with anti-inflammatory effects. Often first-line systemic therapy for lichen planopilaris.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for lichen planopilaris and oral LP — 50-70% stabilization or improvement. Requires ophthalmologic monitoring for retinal toxicity (annual OCT).

medicalTreatments.name: Oral JAK inhibitors — Tofacitinib 5-10mg BID for refractory lichen planopilaris
medicalTreatments.description: For severe, refractory lichen planopilaris not responding to conventional therapy. JAK inhibitors block the IFN-gamma-driven T-cell attack on hair follicles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Promising early data — 50-70% stabilization or improvement in lichen planopilaris. Requires monitoring for infections and cytopenias.

surgicalOptions.name: There are no standard surgical options for lichen planus
surgicalOptions.description: Lichen planus is managed medically. Surgery is not indicated for active inflammatory LP. For lichen planopilaris scarring alopecia, hair transplantation is not appropriate while disease is active — only after disease is completely stabilized.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antihistamines for pruritus control
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-sedating and sedating antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, hydroxyzine) provide symptomatic relief of pruritus, which is often the most bothersome symptom. Particularly useful at night for sleep disruption from itching.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Does not treat the underlying inflammation but significantly improves quality of life by reducing pruritus and allowing sleep.

alternativeTreatments.name: Phototherapy — Narrowband UVB, PUVA
alternativeTreatments.description: UVB and PUVA (psoralen + UVA) have anti-inflammatory effects effective for widespread cutaneous LP. Used for generalized or treatment-resistant LP.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate efficacy for generalized LP — 50-60% improvement. Used when systemic therapy is not desired or as adjunct to other treatments.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dental material replacement for oral LP
alternativeTreatments.description: For oral LP possibly related to amalgam dental fillings, replacement with mercury-free composite or ceramic restorations may improve symptoms in affected patients. Supported by patch testing demonstrating mercury sensitivity.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in a subset of patients with documented contact allergy to dental materials. Not all patients benefit.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be seen for: any new lichen planus-like eruption (for accurate diagnosis); oral lichen planus (for baseline biopsy, monitoring, and management); any signs of lichen planopilaris (for early treatment to prevent permanent hair loss); hypertrophic LP (requires intralesional injections or systemic therapy); widespread or severe LP (requires prescription therapy); and any non-healing oral or genital erosions (to rule out malignant transformation). General practitioners can manage mild, localized cutaneous LP with topical steroids but should refer to a dermatologist for anything beyond mild disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with expertise in inflammatory skin conditions — most can manage cutaneous LP. For oral LP, a dermatologist with experience managing mucosal disease (or co-management with an oral medicine specialist) is optimal. For lichen planopilaris, a dermatologist with specific interest in hair loss disorders is ideal. Ask about their approach to the specific variant you have and what systemic therapy options they offer for more severe disease.
preventionStrategies: Avoid known triggering medications — if a medication is suspected to trigger lichen planus, discuss alternatives with your prescribing physician
Replace amalgam dental fillings (if patch-tested positive for mercury allergy) — for oral LP with documented mercury contact allergy, replacement may reduce disease activity
Treat Hepatitis C — if you have both HCV and LP, treating the HCV may improve the LP; direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) have made HCV treatment highly effective
Minimize skin trauma — Koebner phenomenon means that scratching, friction, and skin injury can trigger new lesions; gentle skincare and prompt treatment of lesions reduces this risk
lifestyleModifications: Use gentle skincare products — avoid harsh soaps, abrasive scrubs, and products that sting or burn; use fragrance-free, gentle cleansers and moisturizers
Avoid excessive scratching — scratching worsens lesions and triggers Koebner phenomenon; keep nails short and use anti-scratch mittens or gloves at night if needed
Use cool compresses for itchy lesions — cool, wet compresses reduce pruritus; topical menthol preparations also provide relief
Wear loose, soft clothing — friction from tight or rough clothing can trigger Koebner phenomenon
Use sunscreen — lichen planus lesions can be photosensitive; broad-spectrum SPF 30+ protects from UV-triggered exacerbations

recommended: Soft, bland, non-spicy foods — for oral LP, spicy, acidic, and rough foods can worsen erosive lesions and cause pain; soft, bland foods are better tolerated
Anti-inflammatory diet — omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish), fruits, vegetables, and whole grains support general immune health
Vitamin D supplementation — vitamin D has immunomodulatory effects; deficiency is common and supplementation may support immune regulation
Iron and B12 supplementation — if deficiency is present; some patients with oral LP have concurrent nutritional deficiencies
avoid: Spicy, acidic, and rough foods — for oral LP, these trigger pain and worsen erosive lesions; citrus fruits, tomatoes, hot spices, and sharp/crunchy foods are poorly tolerated
Alcohol — for oral LP, alcohol can worsen mucosal inflammation and pain; alcohol-containing mouthwashes should also be avoided
Hot beverages and foods — for oral LP, heat can trigger pain; cool or room-temperature foods and drinks are better tolerated
Tobacco — smoking worsens oral LP and increases risk of malignant transformation; cessation is strongly recommended

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is beneficial and recommended for lichen planus patients. No specific restrictions apply. However, friction from exercise equipment (weights, rowing machines) can trigger Koebner phenomenon — use protective padding and soft fabrics. For hypertrophic LP on the shins, exercises that involve repetitive impact (running) may worsen lesions. Swimming is an excellent low-friction option. Shower and apply any prescribed topical medications after exercise.
dailyManagement: Apply topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors as prescribed — consistency is critical for efficacy; apply twice daily to affected areas
Take antihistamines at night for pruritus — particularly useful for sleep disruption from itching
Use gentle, fragrance-free skincare products — avoid harsh soaps and abrasive treatments that can trigger Koebner phenomenon
Attend regular follow-up appointments — for oral LP, regular monitoring (every 6-12 months) is essential to detect any dysplasia or early malignant transformation
Perform oral self-examinations — for oral LP, examine the mouth monthly for non-healing erosions, white patches that are thickening, or any ulcer that persists beyond 2 weeks
Avoid known triggers — medications, dental materials (if tested positive), and aggressive dental procedures that cause mucosal trauma
prognosis: Cutaneous lichen planus has a favorable prognosis in most patients — it is self-limited, with 60-80% of patients experiencing spontaneous remission within 1-2 years. However, recurrences occur in up to 20-40% of patients. Mucosal lichen planus (oral, genital) tends to be more chronic and relapsing, often persisting for years or decades. Oral LP carries a small but real risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma (0.5-2% over decades) — regular monitoring is essential. Lichen planopilaris causes permanent scarring alopecia if not treated early — the goal is to halt disease activity before significant follicle destruction occurs. Hypertrophic LP on the shins is more chronic and difficult to treat than classic cutaneous LP. With appropriate treatment, most patients achieve good symptom control.
recoveryTimeline: With topical corticosteroids (cutaneous LP): improvement in 2-4 weeks; controlled at 4-8 weeks. With systemic corticosteroids: rapid improvement within days; tapered over 2-4 weeks. With oral retinoids: improvement at 4-8 weeks; maximal at 3-6 months. With hydroxychloroquine (lichen planopilaris, oral LP): improvement at 6-12 weeks; maximal at 6 months. With phototherapy: improvement at 4-8 weeks; maximal at 3-6 months. Skin lesions may leave post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation for months after resolution.
complications: Chronic mucosal disease — oral and genital LP can persist for years, causing pain, impaired eating/sexual function, and reduced quality of life
Malignant transformation of oral LP — erosive oral LP carries a 0.5-2% risk of squamous cell carcinoma over decades; requires regular monitoring and biopsy of any non-healing areas
Scarring from genital LP — vulvar and penile LP can cause scarring, architectural changes, and sexual dysfunction; requires vigilant management
Permanent hair loss from lichen planopilaris — scarring alopecia from follicular destruction; early treatment is critical to prevent permanent follicle loss
Nail dystrophy — nail LP can cause permanent nail damage including pterygium (scarring), splitting, and dystrophy; early treatment may prevent permanent damage
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation — lesions may leave brown marks after resolution, particularly in darker-skinned individuals; can persist for months
Koebner phenomenon — new lesions forming at trauma sites; can be triggered by scratching, friction, or medical procedures

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Lichen Planus Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the largest dermatologic professional organization.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS: Lichen Planus. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2023.
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary with evidence-based management recommendations.

supportResources.name: British Society for Oral Medicine — Oral Lichen Planus Resources
supportResources.description: Information and resources for oral lichen planus patients from the British Society for Oral Medicine.

confusedWithConditions.name: Guttate Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Guttate psoriasis presents with rain-drop shaped, erythematous papules and plaques on the trunk and extremities, not the purple, polygonal, flat-topped papules of lichen planus. Guttate psoriasis has silvery (not Wickham's) scales and is not typically pruritic. Both can be triggered by streptococcal infection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczematous Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema presents with erythematous, scaly, eczematous patches rather than discrete, polygonal papules. Eczema is more acute and less violaceous. Lichen planus has the characteristic '6 Ps' and Wickham's striae.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pemphigus Vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pemphigus vulgaris presents with fragile blisters and erosions, not the solid papules of lichen planus. Pemphigus involves oral mucosa before skin in most cases, and the blisters are flaccid and rupture easily. Biopsy confirms the distinction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus Erythematosus (Cutaneous)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Discoid lupus shows well-defined, erythematous, scaly plaques with follicular plugging, atrophy, and telangiectasias; Wickham's striae are absent. Lupus lesions are photosensitive and do not have the purple, polygonal papules of LP. Direct immunofluorescence distinguishes the two.

relatedConditions.name: Lichen Planopilaris
relatedConditions.relevance: The follicular variant of lichen planus affecting the scalp. Causes scarring alopecia and is managed differently from cutaneous LP. Requires early treatment to prevent permanent hair loss.

relatedConditions.name: Oral Lichen Planus
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common mucosal variant, affecting the oral mucosa. Chronic, painful (in erosive form), and carries a small risk of malignant transformation. Requires regular monitoring.

faqs.question: What is the classic presentation of lichen planus?
faqs.answer: The classic presentation is the '6 Ps': Purple, Polygonal, Planar (flat-topped), Pruritic Papules and Plaques. Lesions are characteristically found on the wrists, forearms, ankles, and lower back. Wickham's striae (white, lacy network on the surface) may be visible. Koebner phenomenon — new lesions appearing at trauma sites — is characteristic. The condition is intensely pruritic in most patients.

faqs.question: What causes lichen planus?
faqs.answer: Lichen planus is caused by a T-cell-mediated attack on basal keratinocytes and mucosal epithelial cells. The trigger for this attack varies: Hepatitis C infection (established trigger), drug-induced (from NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides, PPIs, and other medications), contact allergens (particularly amalgam dental materials for oral LP), and unknown autoantigens (most cases). It is not contagious and not caused by poor hygiene.

faqs.question: Is lichen planus genetic?
faqs.answer: Lichen planus has a modest genetic component — approximately 1-2% of cases are familial. HLA associations (HLA-DR1, HLA-DR6, HLA-DR10) have been identified in various populations. Most cases are sporadic with no clear family history. Having a family member with lichen planus slightly increases your risk but does not determine destiny.

faqs.question: Is lichen planus contagious?
faqs.answer: No — lichen planus is absolutely not contagious. It cannot be transmitted through physical contact, sharing of objects, or any other form of exposure. It is an immune-mediated condition caused by the body's T cells attacking its own epithelial cells.

faqs.question: What is oral lichen planus?
faqs.answer: Oral lichen planus affects the oral mucosa — most commonly the buccal mucosa, tongue, and gingiva. It presents as white, lacy (reticular) streaks (the most common and least symptomatic form) or as painful red, atrophic patches and erosions (more symptomatic). Erosive oral LP can significantly impair eating. Oral LP is chronic and carries a small but real risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, requiring regular monitoring.

faqs.question: What is lichen planopilaris?
faqs.answer: Lichen planopilaris is the scarring alopecia variant of lichen planus — inflammation attacks hair follicles on the scalp, causing permanent hair loss. It presents with perifollicular erythema and scaling, progressing to smooth, scarred scalp without visible follicles. Early treatment is critical — once follicles are destroyed, hair loss is permanent. Treatments include topical and intralesional corticosteroids, hydroxychloroquine, and in refractory cases, JAK inhibitors.

faqs.question: What treatments are available for lichen planus?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on site and severity. For localized cutaneous LP: topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, or intralesional corticosteroids. For widespread or severe cutaneous LP: oral corticosteroids (short-term), oral retinoids (acitretin), or phototherapy. For oral LP: topical corticosteroids (gel or mouthwash), calcineurin inhibitors, and for severe cases, systemic therapy. For lichen planopilaris: topical/intralesional corticosteroids, hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, and JAK inhibitors for refractory cases.

faqs.question: Does lichen planus resolve on its own?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous (skin) lichen planus often resolves spontaneously within 1-2 years in most patients — 60-80% experience remission. However, recurrences occur in up to 20-40% of patients. Mucosal lichen planus (oral, genital) tends to be more chronic, often persisting for years or decades. Lichen planopilaris (scarring scalp LP) is chronic and progressive — without treatment, it causes permanent hair loss.

metaTitle: Lichen Planus: Types, Causes, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Lichen planus: itchy purple, polygonal papules and plaques. Learn about causes (Hepatitis C, drugs), types (cutaneous, oral, lichen planopilaris), treatments, and prognosis.

images.altText: Clinical presentation of lichen planus showing characteristic purple, polygonal, flat-topped papules on the forearm
images.caption: Lichen planus typically presents with purple, polygonal, flat-topped, pruritic papules — the '6 Ps'.

images.altText: Oral lichen planus showing white, lacy reticular pattern on the buccal mucosa
images.caption: Oral lichen planus characteristically presents with white, lacy (reticular) streaks on the buccal mucosa.

images.altText: Histological section of lichen planus showing the characteristic band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction and sawtooth acanthosis
images.caption: Lichen planus histology shows a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction and irregular acanthosis (sawtooth pattern).
